title: Automation, networking and innovation spotlighted at Hort Connections 2026
summary:
The topics of automation, networking and innovation were at the forefront of Hort Connections 2026 earlier this month at the Adelaide Convention Centre. The event brought together growers, supply chain partners, retailers, researchers and industry figures from across the global agriculture industry for a four-day programme of speaker sessions, trade shows, demonstrations and discussions. A panel dedicated to agricultural scholarships spoke about the importance of investing in capability and development, spotlighting programmes like the Agrifutures Horizon Scholarship, Australian Rural Leadership Programme and Nuffield Australia Scholarship. On the trade show floor, Daily Wine News had the chance to speak with primary suppliers of the winemaking and grapegrowing industry, including Visy, John Deere, Mottech Parkland, Yates Hort & Ag and Eurosolids.

The post Automation, networking and innovation spotlighted at Hort Connections 2026 appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
By Kyra Trussell

The topics of automation, networking and innovation were at the forefront of Hort Connections 2026 earlier this month at the Adelaide Convention Centre.

The event brought together growers, supply chain partners, retailers, researchers and industry figures from across the global agriculture industry for a four-day programme of speaker sessions, trade shows, demonstrations and discussions.

A panel dedicated to agricultural scholarships spoke on the importance of investing in capability and development, spotlighting programmes like the Agrifutures Horizon Scholarship, Australian Rural Leadership Programme and Nuffield Australia Scholarship.

James Haslett, an emerging Horizon Scholar, talked about how the possibilities for his future attracted him to the scholarship.

“A student wouldn’t really be able to have these opportunities and connect with industry without a little bit of additional help, so who knows – maybe five, 10, 15 years down the track after the conclusion of the programme – who knows what opportunities will present themselves? I find that really valuable,” Haslett said.

Julia Strang explained the Australian Rural Leadership Programme and how it can help leaders be better prepared to tackle industry challenges.

“It brings together about 30 leaders from different industries and communities, and it takes them through a series of intensive, real-world experiences, both across Australia and internationally.

“I think what makes it unique is its depth of experience. It’s not just about skills, it’s about developing self-awareness, building the ability to lead through complexity and creating a strong lifelong network of leaders shaping the future of regional Australia,” she said.

LTR: Courtney Lean and Julia Strang

Courtney Lean, one of the scholars undertaking the programme this year, said she is excited to start and encouraged others to “just apply”.

“I think I would really encourage anyone that’s looking at any of these programmes to challenge their own idea of what a leader is…you might not be in a typical leadership role or a management position or whatever that may look like to you in your field – but if you’re someone that’s excited, you’re engaged and passionate with what it is that you’re doing, you want to make change, you’re open-minded, and just willing to learn and grow, then please apply, because that’s the sort of person that they’re looking for. You’d get a lot out of it,” she said.

CEO of Nuffield Australia and 2015 scholar Andy Clarke spoke about how the programme allows scholars to find a problem they want to solve, then think of and present solutions.

“This is where a grower or a primary producer or somebody aligned to primary production has the ability to go and actually delve into something that’s really impactful for their sector and hopefully provide solutions coming back to bring forward as well,” he said.

On the trade show floor, Daily Wine News had the chance to speak with primary suppliers of the winemaking and grapegrowing industry.

Visy, who offers sustainable packaging solutions, was in attendance.

Sales manager Joshua Romanowicz said that the company is proud to support the industry.

“Visy is one of Australia’s largest privately owned family companies, and we pride ourselves on closing the loop for packaging in Australia. We believe materials are not recycled until they’ve been made into new products. We are investing to increase the recycled content in our glass, to get to an average of 70% recycled content,” he explained.

Romanowicz spoke of Visy’s latest development, its fibre-based insulation liner, Visycell, which is designed to replace EPS (polystyrene).

“Visycell is made from cardboard offcuts. We make an insulation pad, which lines the inside of a cardboard box and keeps the thermal properties there for e-commerce and long-haul deliveries. It’s an exciting innovation, and we continue to support the wine industry to be sustainable in the years to come.”

John Deere was also exhibiting at the event, showcasing its machinery and technological developments designed “to make operations more efficient”.

Erin Wagstaff, marketing manager at John Deere, spoke of the company’s cloud-based platform ‘Operations Centre’, which can store machine data, records and documentation for sprayers. The company also develops auto-steering systems alongside its well-known tractors.

“We love attending Hort Connections, I think it’s one of the best events on the horticultural calendar for the year. Talking to growers is one of the most important things for us. I think it’s really great for us to hear what’s on their mind, hear what’s going on in the industry, what are the trends that they’re getting on board with, so we can be aware and try to prepare for that.

“I think just the networking in general too, being able to keep up with those industry trends is really essential, and understanding what’s top of mind,” Wagstaff shared.

Andy Sawell from Mottech Parkland worked as an irrigation manager in a vineyard for eight years before starting his role with the irrigation systems provider.

LTR: Doron Halosh and Andy Sawell

“This role helped me understand what happens on the other side in the vineyard and really understand the needs, the trials and the issues that [vineyard irrigation] managers face…I understand how our products can really benefit the vineyards in that day-to-day process,” he revealed.

Mottech Parkland offers technology for automation of irrigation systems throughout the vineyard and wineries for wastewater distribution.

“The importance of automation going forward for wineries during this downturn is the labour saving, where we can save money for the business and reduce labour.

“Our technology allows vineyards to automate and also have feedback from sensors indicating that the block is actually on. It’s very useful during summer when you’re short-staffed, to know that your vineyard is being watered accordingly as you’ve programmed it to go.

“The new features of our Elite range offer a Wi-Fi network that enables Wi-Fi throughout the whole property, and the backup of that is in areas that have low phone signal, there is a safety option for your operators to use that Wi-Fi network to make calls and use the phone app to operate their irrigation,” Sawell detailed.

As for being at Hort Connections 2026, Sawell said the event was fantastic for showcasing the company’s new products to a whole range of audiences.

The downturn of the wine industry was a topic of conversation echoed by Christian Hubener, a French agronomist and crop consultant who manned the Eurosolids stand.

“In the past, wine was an easy thing to just produce and sell. Everybody was happy with it!”

Hubener spoke at length about the pressures climate change, declining consumption, changing consumer habits and the NoLo trend are placing on the industry.

“What we (Eurosolids) try to do is actually to help the grapegrowers to manage higher yield in order to have better balance in harvest and in the wine at the end of the season. The idea is really to introduce tools like fertiliser and biostimulants to achieve the goals that they might have with this new creation we’ve made for the wine industry,” he explained.

Chris Lloyd, McLaren Vale local and area sales manager for Yates Hort & Ag, expressed similar sentiments.

“I do feel for the industry, because as a whole, for many years now, the bulk side of the industry has added an excessive amount of pressure, and there’s multiple facets of the pressure, it’s not just one thing,” he shared.

Lloyd went on to explain the offerings from Yates Hort & Ag that can benefit grapegrowers, including its ‘Ecocarb PLUS’ – which can be used for powdery mildew suppression and as an eco-protector for botrytis.

He emphasised the importance of keeping plants healthy and using kelp to strengthen vines.

“The thinking should be if we keep kelp in the programme, we’re going to keep a healthy vine, we’re going to get healthy root systems, we’re going to be able to access more nutrition and water from a healthy root architecture.

“Healthy plants are less susceptible to insect and pest and disease attacks because of the strength of the cell walls, of the leaves – the most vulnerable part of the plant – and there’s the protection that the kelp brings to the vines that, not only do you get that increased efficacy of nutrition, but you can also get the benefits of reduction in use of pesticide and fungicide,” Lloyd emphasised.

Having attended Hort Connections at least three times prior to this year, he described the event as a key opportunity for the wholesale business to connect with its customer base of resellers at the event and catch up on any pending business.

“I always find that Hort Connections is the premiere event for the year because of the amount of people that it attracts, not only as exhibitors, but also as attendees. Being a local person, I get to see a lot of my mates in one place, so there’s always that good connection back to people that you may have not seen for a while – whether they be ex-employees or they’re just mates within the industry.

“We find it’s a great group of people and it’s a lot of camaraderie.”

title: “The results of a lifetime” – A ‘very special’ tasting event and the future of Gippsland wine
summary:
A wine tasting event set to uncork a collection of East Gippsland wines from the 1980s to the 2020 vintage will be held at the Nicholson River Winery’s barrel room this weekend. The event will explore the question, “Do East Gippsland wines age well?” across two sessions. Saturday will involve predominantly white and some red wines, while Sunday will focus exclusively on reds. The wines have mostly been made from grapes grown at Nicholson River, however, the occasional wine from other parts of Gippsland will also feature. Gippsland wine pioneer Ken Eckersley, who planted his first vines with his wife Juliet in 1978, described the event as an opportunity to experience “the results of a lifetime” of winemaking.

The post “The results of a lifetime” – A ‘very special’ tasting event and the future of Gippsland wine appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
The tastings, set to take place from 20-21 of June, will feature wines from as far back as the 80s. Image supplied

By Kyra Trussell

A wine tasting event set to uncork a collection of East Gippsland wines from the 1980s to the 2020 vintage will be held at the Nicholson River Winery’s barrel room this weekend.

The event will explore the question, “Do East Gippsland wines age well?” across two sessions. Saturday will involve predominantly white and some red wines, while Sunday will focus exclusively on reds.

The wines have mostly been made from grapes grown at Nicholson River, however, the occasional wine from other parts of Gippsland will also feature.

Gippsland wine pioneer Ken Eckersley, who planted his first vines with his wife Juliet in 1978, described the event as an opportunity to experience “the results of a lifetime” of winemaking.

“We’re getting too old now, and we’re looking to retire, so we’re looking back at over a lifetime of wine that we’ve made. I think we’ve improved our winemaking a lot over the years, I think it’s a very exciting area and we’ve been able to make reasonable alcohol, high flavour wines…it’s the results of a lifetime.

“These are wines I’ve kept in the cellar and I’m pulling them out over that weekend to a limited number of lucky people and we will taste them.

“This is sort of a history of Gippsland wines being tasted, it doesn’t get any better than that!” he exclaimed.

Eckersley will speak on each wine at the event, joking that “we’re spreading the gospel of wine – we’re missionaries here.”

The wine journey hasn’t been a walk in the park for the couple, with Eckersley expressing that the couple has had to “fight for the grapes”.

“We’ve had lots of pressures, sometimes drought, sometimes floods, plenty of birds –this is Australia, the home of birds – and so we’ve had to work hard. It hasn’t fallen into our lap; we don’t just sit under the vine and suck straws and watch the grapes grow,” he laughed.

As for what makes Gippsland wine so special, Eckersley highlighted its flavours.

“Gippsland is actually a big place, there are several different climate zones, and in the wine industry we’re fairly small, but I think what we have is really good flavours, distinctive flavours as our standout thing. That’s important, because I think a lot of Australian wines going down in flavour with the effect of climate change and so on.

“Here on the coast, where we’ve got a very mild climate, very long season, we get wonderful flavour development, we don’t have to leave them hanging on because they’re overripe.”

He added that people are willing to pay “very good money” for the grapes, as much as $8,000 a tonne.

When asked why the region is so underrated in his opinion, the winery owner said that the area “lacks confidence in itself, frankly”.

“It’s diverse, there’s timber, sheep, cattle, a little bit of horticulture, little bit of tourism, and it could do with a signature product, and wine has got that capacity, I think.

“I think the older areas, which were really chosen for Port and Sherry, are really suffering a lot from climate change. Here and in Tasmania, we’re in the same situation where the future is more likely to be here in these coastal places which are just starting to appear. People are just starting to notice that the future is more likely to lie with the newer areas than the old, tired areas.

“I think there’s a lot of potential here, for example, for more of the Italian varieties like Fiano and Vermentino and so on.”

He also pointed to the nature of the wine industry itself, emphasising its traditionalism that he believes tends to overshadow smaller regions.

“I think it’s a very conservative industry, too conservative, actually… I mean, it holds itself back, and I think there’s just been a failure to really notice or take up that what’s happening in the new areas in the last 30 to 40 years. It’s a highly competitive industry with the big boys and the big areas, traditional ones.

“I think South Australia is pretty insulated from what’s happening in wine and the rest of Australia, frankly. Everyone in Adelaide thinks they’re the centre of the wine universe, and that’s really delusional.

“There is a big wine world!” he concluded.

Tickets and more information on the tasting event are available via the East Gippsland Winter Festival website.

title: Château Tanunda secures two Top 10 spots in global Shiraz ranking
summary:
Barossa’s Château Tanunda has had two of its flagship wines ranked among the Top 10 Best Shiraz in the World at the Syrah du Monde 2026 competition in France. Grand Barossa Shiraz 2024 and 100 Year Old Vines Shiraz 2022 made the ranking, beating out international entries from 20 wine-producing nations and earning two Gold medals. “Château Tanunda's success not only reflects the exceptional quality of its wines but also reinforces the Barossa's reputation as one of the world's leading regions for Shiraz,” said managing director Michelle Geber.

The post Château Tanunda secures two Top 10 spots in global Shiraz ranking appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
Château Tanunda has received global recognition via the ranking. Image supplied

Barossa’s Château Tanunda has had two of its flagship wines ranked among the Top 10 Best Shiraz in the World at the Syrah du Monde 2026 competition in France.

Grand Barossa Shiraz 2024 and 100 Year Old Vines Shiraz 2022 made the ranking, beating out international entries from 20 wine-producing nations and earning two Gold medals.

“Château Tanunda’s success not only reflects the exceptional quality of its wines but also reinforces the Barossa’s reputation as one of the world’s leading regions for Shiraz,” said managing director Michelle Geber.

“The Grand Barossa range is one of our most internationally recognised collections, so receiving recognition at a global competition such as Syrah du Monde is incredibly rewarding. Achievements like this continue to build Australia’s reputation as a producer of exceptional Shiraz.”

The winery earlier this year released the Grand Barossa Shiraz 2024 in a new 375ml format in a bid to “suit a wider range of consumers and occasions”.

Château Tanunda said that its 100 Year Old Vines Shiraz 2022 symbolises its “commitment to preserving and championing Barossa’s unique old-vine heritage”. The winery also added that, as a “proud member” of the Francs de Pied association, it supports the recognition and protection of rare own-rooted vineyards around the world.

The Château Tanunda 100 Year Old Vines Shiraz 2022 Retails for RRP $180, while the Grand Barossa Shiraz 2024 retails for RRP $25. The wines are available for purchase online via the winery’s website or in-person nationally at fine wine retailers.

title: Misha’s Vineyard Wines announces sale of Bendigo Vineyard, assures “business as usual”
summary:
Misha's Vineyard Wines has announced the sale of its 57-hectare Bendigo vineyard property. The winery said this decision marks “an important new phase” for the Central Otago wine producer “while ensuring continuity of wine production through a multi-year grape supply agreement”. Under the terms of the agreement, Misha's Vineyard Wines will continue to receive grapes from the Bendigo vineyard, which has been at the heart of the company’s wines since its inception, under a multi-year supply arrangement. According to Misha’s Vineyard Wines, the transaction will enable the company to focus on crafting and marketing its premium Central Otago wines which are reportedly enjoyed in 16 markets around the world. "This is a positive and strategic step for our business," said Andy Wilkinson, co-founder and director of Misha's Vineyard Wines.

The post Misha’s Vineyard Wines announces sale of Bendigo Vineyard, assures “business as usual” appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
The Bendigo vineyard has been sold. Image courtesy Misha’s Vineyard Wines via Facebook

Misha’s Vineyard Wines has announced the sale of its 57-hectare Bendigo vineyard property.

The winery said this decision marks “an important new phase” for the Central Otago wine producer “while ensuring continuity of wine production through a multi-year grape supply agreement”.

Under the terms of the agreement, Misha’s Vineyard Wines will continue to receive grapes from the Bendigo vineyard, which has been at the heart of the company’s wines since its inception, under a multi-year supply arrangement.

According to Misha’s Vineyard Wines, the transaction will enable the company to focus on crafting and marketing its premium Central Otago wines which are reportedly enjoyed in 16 markets around the world.

“This is a positive and strategic step for our business,” said Andy Wilkinson, co-founder and director of Misha’s Vineyard Wines.

“Our focus has always been on producing exceptional wines and building our brand in markets around the world. This transaction allows us to continue doing exactly that, while working alongside experienced wine industry owners who share our commitment to quality and the long-term future of the vineyard.”

Wilkinson believes the sale reflects confidence in the future of New Zealand’s premium wine sector.

“We are delighted with the outcome and have every confidence in the new owners as custodians of the vineyard.

“Their experience in producing premium New Zealand wines and commitment to quality gives us confidence that the vineyard will continue to thrive for many years to come.”

The winery said that for customers and trade partners, it’s business as usual. The company affirmed that it will continue to operate independently, producing and marketing its wines under the Misha’s Vineyard brand as well as “maintaining its relationships with customers and distribution partners around the world”.

“The vineyard has always been an important part of our story, and that story continues.

“As we celebrate our 20th vintage, our customers can look forward to the same wines, the same commitment to quality, and the same passion that has defined Misha’s Vineyard Wines from the beginning,” Wilkinson reassured.

title: Central Otago’s organics shift celebrated at national awards
summary:
Central Otago’s shift towards organic wine has been recognised at the recent Aotearoa New Zealand Organic Wine Awards, with several of the region’s wineries receiving accolades. Of the 181 medals awarded, 73 went to 11 Central Otago wineries, making for 40% of the national total. Gibbston Valley was a strong performer at the awards, securing six gold medals and the highest total medal count. Gibbston Valley chief winemaker Christopher Keys said the results reflect both vineyard excellence and the strong trajectory of the wider industry. “It is a joy to see the consistent excellence New Zealand’s organic vineyards have reached. “This is recognition not just for Gibbston Valley, but for New Zealand organic wine as a whole. It’s one part of a bigger picture, but it is an important part,” Keys said.

The post Central Otago’s organics shift celebrated at national awards appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
Christopher Keys, chief winemaker at Gibbston Valley. Images supplied

Central Otago’s shift towards organic wine has been recognised at the recent Aotearoa New Zealand Organic Wine Awards, with several of the region’s wineries receiving accolades.

Of the 181 medals awarded, 73 went to 11 Central Otago wineries, making for 40% of the national total.

Gibbston Valley was a strong performer at the awards, securing six gold medals and the highest total medal count.

Gibbston Valley chief winemaker Christopher Keys said the results reflect both vineyard excellence and the strong trajectory of the wider industry.

“It is a joy to see the consistent excellence New Zealand’s organic vineyards have reached.

“This is recognition not just for Gibbston Valley, but for New Zealand organic wine as a whole. It’s one part of a bigger picture, but it is an important part,” Keys said.

Central Otago Winegrowers Association general manager Carolyn Murray welcomed the success of Central Otago producers at the awards.

“With 30% of our vineyard area now under organic management, Central Otago has become New Zealand’s leading organic wine region.

“This achievement reflects the commitment of growers who believe that great wine begins in the vineyard – with healthy soils, balanced ecosystems, and a deep respect for the land,” she said.

According to the 2025 New Zealand Winegrowers Sustainability Report, 16 per cent of wineries now hold organic certification, up from 10 per cent in 2024.

Keys said this growth is “translating into quality and character in the glass”.

“Being organic brings you closer to the place, revealing the real identity of the wine. When you open a bottle, it has the ability to transport you back to this special part of the world.”

Now in its 13th year, the Aotearoa New Zealand Organic Wine Awards celebrate the country’s best certified organic wines. To be eligible, entrants must use certified organic grapes and are assessed through a blind tasting process by “an independent, consumer-based judging panel”.

Gibbston Valley’s top honours were led by its single-vineyard Pinot Noir’s, which featured strongly in the gold medal tally, alongside the 2025 rosé and 2024 Chardonnay.

Other major award winners included Greystone as ‘Riedel Vineyard of the Year’, Mount Edward Muirkirk pinot noir 2024 as ‘Riedel Wine of the Show’ and Quartz Reef Wines as ‘Sustainable Vineyard of the Year’.

Gibbston Valley executive managing director Cristina Griffith said the results underscore the winery’s long-term investment in organic practices.

“These awards highlight not only how outstanding the wines are, but also the commitment behind converting our vineyards without losing an ounce of quality.”

title: North Island and Hawke’s Bay Young Viticulturist of the Year winners revealed
summary:
Heath Miller has been announced as the 2026 Osmond Nurseries Young Viticulturist of the Year for Hawke’s Bay, whilst Alex Coombs took out the title in North Island. It is the third time Miller has entered the competition, a feat that New Zealand Winegrowers (NZW) said highlights his determination. Nicky Grandorge, leadership & communities manager at NZW, said that the organisation loves seeing contestants come back year after year. “It highlights the passion they have to progress themselves and the importance of the competition,” Grandorge said. It was also North Island winner Alex Coombs’ third year competing in the competition. Coombs’ participation followed a year overseas, which reportedly equipped him with more viticultural experience and a “strong focus and determination to take out the title”.

The post North Island and Hawke’s Bay Young Viticulturist of the Year winners revealed appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
LTR: Alex Coombs from Craggy Range and Heath Miller from Pask. Images supplied

Heath Miller has been announced as the 2026 Osmond Nurseries Young Viticulturist of the Year for Hawke’s Bay, whilst Alex Coombs took out the title in North Island.

It is the third time Miller has entered the competition, a feat that New Zealand Winegrowers (NZW) said highlights his determination.

Nicky Grandorge, leadership & communities manager at NZW, said that the organisation loves seeing contestants come back year after year.

“It highlights the passion they have to progress themselves and the importance of the competition,” Grandorge said.

The organisation also extended congratulations to runner ups Thomas Mackenzie from Indevin and Thalia Osborne from Ash Ridge Wines. Mackenzie was the first person to register for the 2026 competitions across the entire country back in January, whilst Osborne represented Hawke’s Bay in last year’s Young Winemaker national final.

Contestants Harsh Bhagran, Ross Graham and Mikaela Salmon were also congratulated for “tackling the challenges with gusto”.

It was also North Island winner Alex Coombs’ third year competing in the competition.

Coombs’ participation followed a year overseas, which reportedly equipped him with more viticultural experience and a “strong focus and determination to take out the title”.

NZW congratulated second-place winner Gagandeep Singh as well as the other two contestants Phattaradanai Wannaphirun (Neil) and Santiago Mendez Gasca for their efforts.

“All four contestants shone in various sections of the competition highlighting their personal strengths,” New Zealand Winegrowers said.

Coombs won both the head-to-head races, the Ecotrellis trellising section, the BioStart Hortisports and the Syngenta Biologicals Nutrition section.

From this regional competition, Miller and Coombs both won a Fruitfed Supplies Field Trip plus $1,000 each and will both compete for “even greater prizes” at the National Final.

content:
Glenn Goodall and Darren Rathbone with the ‘Prime Minister’s Trophy for Champion of Wine Show’. Images supplied

Wineries around the country are celebrating prestigious trophy wins at last night’s Canberra ceremony for the National Wine Show.

Adelaide Hills producer Sidewood Estate took home the ‘Best Sauvignon Blanc Trophy’ for its 2025 Sauvignon Blanc.

This win is the third time the family-owned winery has claimed the same trophy, following previous wins in 2019 and 2021. The winery said that, based on published results dating back to 2012, it is the only producer to have secured three victories for its Sauvignon Blanc.

Owner and vigneron for Sidewood Estate Owen Inglis said that the pattern of winning is no mistake.

“A single trophy rewards one outstanding vintage. A record-breaking three trophies? Well, that speaks to exceptional vineyards and expert winemaking.

“Three trophies across six shows say more than any one vintage could. It tells you the fruit, the sites and the team are holding their standard, season after season. That consistency is what we set out to build, and it’s what the judges continue to recognise,” Inglis said.

Overall, South Australia was the most successful state for wins, taking home nine of the show’s 23 trophies. It was also well ahead of other Australian states for gold, silver or bronze medals. South Australia notched up 292 medals, compared with next-placed NSW with 155.

Among the two NSW trophy winners was the Canberra Region’s Sapling Yard, which won the ‘Blended White Trophy’ for its 2025 Fiori Riesling Pinot Gris Gewürztraminer Pinot Blanc and was also a finalist in the Shiraz Blends category.

McWilliam’s Wines are celebrating having received two Gold medals for its McWilliam’s 2024 Single Vineyard Chardonnay and it’s McWilliam’s NV Hanwood Estate 20 Year Old Rare Muscat.

McWilliam’s (Calabria Family Wines) chief winemaker Emma Norbiato said the team are thrilled to have been awarded the medals.

“This recognition is a proud reflection of the dedication, passion and craftsmanship that goes into every bottle, from vineyard to winery. Our McWilliam’s Single Vineyard Chardonnay is a pure expression of terroir, showcasing the unique character of Tumbarumba, while the Hanwood Estate 20 Year Old Rare Muscat is a true labour of love, patiently nurtured over two decades.

“These awards are a testament to the incredible team whose commitment to quality, character, and authenticity continues to drive everything we do. We are honoured to share this achievement with everyone who has supported our journey,” she said.

Victoria’s three awards included this year’s ‘Sparkling Trophy’, previously a stronghold of Tasmanian winemakers, for Coldstream Hills’ 2018 Yarra Valley Pinot Noir Chardonnay.

Western Australia’s Margaret River region has a lot to celebrate after it received the competition’s top three trophies.

Xanadu’s 2023 Cabernet Sauvignon won the ‘Prime Minister’s Trophy for Champion Wine of Show’, with regional neighbour Evans & Tate’s 2023 Single Vineyard Chardonnay as the runner up.

To compete for the top award, both wines earlier secured the ‘James Halliday Trophy for Best Red Wine of Show’ and the ‘Len Evans Memorial Trophy for Best White’ respectively.

Margaret River contributed strongly to WA’s overall seven-trophy haul. All three finalists in the Cabernet Sauvignon class – first-time exhibitor La Kooki, Xanadu and Passel Estate – come from the south-west WA region.

While Xanadu claimed the Cabernet trophy for the 10th time in 13 years, La Kooki picked up the White Varietal Trophy for its 2025 Chenin Rocks Chenin Blanc. It was another coup for Xanadu’s senior winemaker Glenn Goodall, who separately runs La Kooki with his winemaker wife Eloise Jarvis.

The 2026 National Wine Show had a record 1085 entries, including 83 first-time exhibitors.

Chair of judges Matt Harrop noted significant growth in the Red and White Varietals categories, recording 25% and 15% more entrants respectively.

“These are turning into a great couple of classes, with so many brilliant wines. It shows the depth of knowledge, understanding and dedication these winemakers are putting into the vineyard.

“There’s commercial pressure on mainstream varietals, with so many available at so many price points, so if you can do something a little distinct, new, slightly different in flavour, it’s good. Grenache used to be in the other varietals category and now it has its own class. Some of the varietals that got to the finals this year, such as Mencia, Gamay or Fiano, have the potential to do the same,” Harrop said.

Harrop said the winning Cabernet Sauvignon was “emblematic of its maker” which, along with fellow 2026 trophy winners Morris Wines, Tyrrells Vineyards and De Bortoli, was a regular fixture on the podium.

“We’ve long known Xanadu makes incredible cab sav, and this one didn’t have a hair out of place. It kept getting good scores, from the first round through to the Cabernet trophy then the red wine of show and finally the PM’s award,” he said.

Harrop also noted that he was delighted to see seven first-time entrants get to the finals, with two of them winning trophies – Lobethal Road (Pinot Gris/Grigio Trophy), and La Kooki (White Varietal Trophy).

“The reason we continue to see record entries in the National Wine Show is that people realise that every wine gets a fair hearing, so anybody can win it.

“We have a great cohort of judges who put a fine-tooth comb over every entry. A lot of shows are too quick to dismiss some wines, but that’s not how we do it. If you produce something exceptional, you’re in with a chance of a trophy, whether you’re a complete unknown or one of Australia’s most famous labels.”

National Wine Show chair Andy Gregory said the record number of first-time entrants was an indication of the esteem in which the National Wine Show was held.

“We are the only truly federated adjudication of the very best in Australian wine. To enter, you must have already won a gold or silver medal in a capital city or qualifying regional show.

“Over the past three years, we’ve introduced more than 200 new exhibitors to Australia’s most important wine awards, which contributes to the breadth and depth of the show. We’re shining a spotlight on exactly what’s happening in Australian winemaking at a particular point in time,” Gregory said.

He added that this year’s winners list once again debunked the myth that in order to drink quality wine you have to pay a premium price.

“The fact that seven of this year’s trophy winners can be purchased for around $30 or less is proof that the best wines in Australia can also be very affordable.”

title: Casella Family Brands enters distribution partnership with Oatley Fine Wine Merchants
summary:
Casella Family Brands (CFB) yesterday announced the appointment of Oatley Fine Wine Merchants as its distribution partner for its premium wine portfolio in Australia from 1 July 2026. The agreement includes the distribution of its Peter Lehmann Wines, Morris of Rutherglen and Brand’s Laira.

The post Casella Family Brands enters distribution partnership with Oatley Fine Wine Merchants appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
Casella Family Brands (CFB) yesterday announced the appointment of Oatley Fine Wine Merchants as its distribution partner for its premium wine portfolio in Australia from 1 July 2026.

The agreement includes the distribution of its Peter Lehmann Wines, Morris of Rutherglen and Brand’s Laira.

CFB said that the agreement joins them to one of the country’s “leading family-owned premium wine distributors, united by a shared commitment to quality, authenticity and long-term brand building”.

The partnership aims to:

  • Expand CFB’s presence in the on-premise sector and the independent retail channel.
  • Leverage Oatley’s strong customer relationships and national distribution scale.
  • Enhance the visibility and relevance of these brands with premium wine consumers.

John Casella, managing director for Casella Family Brands, said that the partnership marks an exciting new chapter for the brands.

“Their deep relationships in on-premise and independent retail, combined with their commitment to family-owned producers, make them a natural fit for these iconic Australian brands.

“On-premise and independent wine stores are vital environments for premium wine, as they’re where consumers discover, experience and engage with brands. Strengthening our presence in these channels is a key driver of long-term brand growth,” Casella said.

“We’re proud to welcome these respected brands to our portfolio. Their heritage and quality align strongly with our philosophy, and we look forward to accelerating their growth with our customers across Australia,” said Sandy Oatley, owner of Oatley Fine Wine Merchants.

CFB said the partnership will also enable the winery to focus its internal resources on driving off-premise growth and core global brands, including [yellow tail], ensuring the business continues to meet the evolving needs of its customers.

title: Tumbarumba wine region gains visibility at industry event
summary:
Tumbarumba was well represented in Sydney last week, with local vignerons Stuart Barclay, Richard Cottam and Elvie Yates attending key wine industry events aimed at shaping the future of Australia's grape and wine sector. The trio joined representatives from wine regions across New South Wales, including the Hunter Valley, Orange, Canberra District, Southern Highlands, Riverina and Shoalhaven Coast, for an industry workshop featuring Wine Australia CEO Martin Cole, Australian Grape & Wine CEO Lee McLean and NSW Wine President Mark Bourne. The workshop explored progress on the industry’s national One Sector Plan, research priorities, consumer trends and advocacy issues affecting grapegrowers and winemakers across the country.

The post Tumbarumba wine region gains visibility at industry event appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
Tumbarumba’s Juliet Cullen with 2026 Sommelier of the Year Shun Eto. Image courtesy Matt Beaver

Tumbarumba was well represented in Sydney last week, with local vignerons Stuart Barclay, Richard Cottam and Elvie Yates attending key wine industry events aimed at shaping the future of Australia’s grape and wine sector.

The trio joined representatives from wine regions across New South Wales, including the Hunter Valley, Orange, Canberra District, Southern Highlands, Riverina and Shoalhaven Coast, for an industry workshop featuring Wine Australia CEO Martin Cole, Australian Grape & Wine CEO Lee McLean and NSW Wine President Mark Bourne.

The workshop explored progress on the industry’s national One Sector Plan, research priorities, consumer trends and advocacy issues affecting grapegrowers and winemakers across the country.

Like many agricultural industries, the wine sector continues to navigate a complex mix of challenges including rising costs, labour shortages, changing consumer preferences, increasing compliance requirements, climatic extremes and uncertainty in global markets.

Among the topics discussed were the development of a National Vineyard Register, opportunities in emerging wine categories such as mid-strength wines, the importance of the domestic market for smaller producers, and the ongoing need for mental health support within regional communities.

Participants also discussed the proposed expansion of Container Deposit Schemes to include wine and spirit bottles from 2027. While the initiative aims to improve glass recycling outcomes and support community fundraising opportunities, concerns remain about the additional administrative and compliance costs that may be borne by smaller wine businesses.

The workshop followed an evening reception at NSW Parliament House, where grapegrowers, winemakers and sommeliers met with members of parliament and industry leaders to discuss issues affecting the sector. Key discussion points included the proposed container deposit changes and potential impacts on land tax exemptions for some primary producers.

The evening also featured the NSW Sommelier Wine List awards, hosted by NSW Wine and Destination NSW. The prestigious Sommelier of the Year Award was won by Shun Eto from Altitude Restaurant at the Shangri-La Sydney.

Eto, along with NSW Wine Outstanding Service Award recipient Angelica Nohra from The Blue Door, previously attended the Tumbarumba Vignerons Association’s Immerse Tumbarumba program in 2022. The initiative brought sommeliers, buyers, winemakers and other wine trade professionals to the region to experience first-hand the people, vineyards and wines that make Tumbarumba unique.

Both have since become strong advocates for the region, with Tumbarumba wines and wines made from Tumbarumba fruit featuring prominently on their wine lists.

“It was wonderful to see familiar faces recognised at the awards,” Yates said.

“What was equally pleasing was that they recognised us too. It is a sign that awareness of the Tumbarumba wine region continues to grow within the industry.”

While Tumbarumba has earned a reputation among wine professionals as one of Australia’s premium cool-climate regions, local representatives say there is still significant opportunity to build recognition with the broader public.

“Events such as these are important opportunities to promote Tumbarumba and ensure our region has a voice in discussions that will influence the future of the Australian wine industry,” Yates said.

summary:

The post Wayfinder goes off-grid with new estate development opening August appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
A render of the entrance and garden. Images supplied

As a part of its “commitment to low intervention winemaking and regenerative farming”, Wayfinder is set to publicly open its new Wilyabrup winery, cellar door and restaurant in August 2026.

Wayfinder said the estate features “energy-efficient architectural design, re-established local flora and a farm-to-table philosophy”.

The existing market garden, which supplies the Wayfinder Wine Bar & Restaurant in Dunsborough, will undergo a “significant expansion”, growing to a full acre. The garden team reportedly plans to grow and harvest “everything from heirloom vegetables to edible flowers”. Wayfinder said the focus will be on seasonal crop rotation, no-dig beds and cover crops to enrich the soil.

Newly hired executive chef Brendan Pratt will lead the on-site restaurant with “a seasonal approach”, where the garden-led menu will build on the Dunsborough Wine Bar & Restaurant concept. Alongside the refreshed menu, the full Wayfinder range of wines will be available by the glass or in tasting flights.

Local WA artist Lajos Vargos, as well as other Australian artists including Lindy Lee, Tjawina Porter, Abie Kemarre Loy and Melissa Kngwarreye Long have been commissioned for various artworks and installations, inspired by the site’s “underpinning connection to the land”.

The new winery build was completed in January 2026 in time for vintage in February.

Designed by Kerry Hill Architects Fremantle, the winery is built underground into a turkey-nest dam wall and powered by solar panels, making Wayfinder fully off the grid.

Wayfinder said the multilevel design of the winery “allows grapes to be received on the ground level, with gentle gravity leading juice below ground to 12 concrete tanks (six 4,500L and six 5,700L), conserving energy and preserving integrity”. It also reportedly benefits from natural temperature control provided by the landscape itself.

Paul McArdle, CEO of Overstory (which owns Wayfinder), explained the new design.

CEO of Overstory Paul McArdle with Andrew Trio, Wayfinder head winemaker.

“From the beginning, Wayfinder’s philosophy has been grounded in environmental responsibility and a deep respect for the land. Every architectural decision has been made to minimise our footprint, with less impact on the surrounding ecosystem,” he said.

Director at Kerry Hill Architects Patrick Kosky said that they wanted the transition from Earth to building to feel seamless, as if the design “emerges from the surrounding landscape”.

“This idea is reflected in the sloping rammed earth wall, natural materials, and the carefully considered landscape design,” he said.

The new winery build was completed in January 2026, in time for vintage in February, while the cellar door soft launch is set to be completed in August.

content:
Oakridge Estate has taken out the top Victorian honour. Images supplied

The 2023 Oakridge Wines 864 Funder & Diamond Drive Block Chardonnay from the Yarra Valley has won the prestigious 2025 Victorian Premier’s Trophy.

The award is judged on a taste-off between winning wines from each of Victoria’s regional wine shows in 2025.

Victorian Wine Show said that this year’s winning wine was selected by a panel of wine industry experts including Joshua Cooper from the Macedon Ranges, Matt Harrop from the Strathbogie Ranges, Steve Flamstead from the Yarra Valley and Jen Pfeiffer from Rutherglen.

Joshua Cooper, chair of judges at the Victorian Wine Show, said that the judging represents the best of the best of the Victorian wine industry.

“The 2023 Oakridge Wines 864 Funder & Diamond Drive Block Chardonnay won in a taste-off from the winners of the 11 regional wine shows and the Victorian Wine Show. It was a stand-out wine from a great vintage which showed varietal typicity combined with intense and sustained depth,” he said.

“The Premier’s Trophy tasting and awards presentation is a celebration of our state’s amazing diversity of wines, vineyards and the people behind them,” said Narelle King, Victorian Wine Show Chair.

Cooper added that the finalists for the award “represent the depth and breadth of this great state for winegrowing and making”.

The awards reception at Parliament House presented the competing wines for a tasting which was attended by members from both houses, industry representatives and the finalists.

Selected Parliamentarians including Michaela Settle, Minister for Agriculture, conducted their own separate tasting “under wine show conditions with guidance from wine show judges” to select the Premier’s Choice Award.

Following “spirited discussion”, the winning wine was awarded to 2024 King River Estate Flying Duck Shiraz from the King Valley. All panellists agreed that the wine displayed “vibrancy with lifted berry characters combined with an approachable silky tannin structure”.

During the presentation of awards, Minister Settle concluded that “it was the diversity of our state from coastal and cooler climate regions producing Chardonnay with varietal character combined with finesse and elegance to inland regions producing fuller-bodied Shiraz with complexity that set Victoria apart as a prominent winegrowing state.”

title: De Bortoli uproots vines in Riverina and Rutherglen
summary:
According to reporting by The Australian Darren De Bortoli has decided to pull out more vines, some up to 40 years old, from vineyards in Riverina and Rutherglen. “It is very tough obviously, it is a continuation of the same saga for the last few years and it is just a question of have we hit the low point yet?” De Bortoli told The Australian. The Australian said that De Bortoli believes the nation’s wine industry is “in a tragic state with no hopes of any near-term improvement”. He told the publication that the vines pulled were mostly Shiraz, with the decrease in red wine consumption a driving factor behind the decision.

The post De Bortoli uproots vines in Riverina and Rutherglen appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
Darren De Bortoli has reportedly made the decision to uproot mostly Shiraz vines. Image supplied

According to reporting by The Australian Darren De Bortoli has decided to pull out more vines, some up to 40 years old, from vineyards in Riverina and Rutherglen.

“It is very tough obviously, it is a continuation of the same saga for the last few years and it is just a question of have we hit the low point yet?” De Bortoli told The Australian.

The Australian said that De Bortoli believes the nation’s wine industry is “in a tragic state with no hopes of any near-term improvement”.

He told the publication that the vines pulled were mostly Shiraz, with the decrease in red wine consumption a driving factor behind the decision.

De Bortoli said to The Australian that lighter wines such as Prosecco in King Valley and other varieties in Yarra Valley were performing better.

De Bortoli isn’t the only winery making the tough choice to pull up vines, with The Australian noting that Tahbilk intentionally burnt vineyards last year consisting of mainly old Shiraz and Chardonnay. The outlet added that Tahbilk intends to continue this purge, with “plans to rip out a number of Shiraz and Cabernet vines”.

In February last year, an ABC reporter interviewed grower Bruno Brombal, who estimated that 3,000 hectares of vines had already been pulled out in the Riverina.

The same article detailed how Riverland grapegrower Mintu Brar was operating at a significant financial loss for the past four years as a result of low prices.

Brar told the ABC that his production costs were a minimum of $300 per tonne, but he only received $125 per tonne from the winery.

The decisions from De Bortoli and Tahbilk demonstrate how even Australia’s top wineries are not immune to the impacts of changing consumer habits and red wine oversupply.

title: Automation, networking and innovation spotlighted at Hort Connections 2026
summary:
The topics of automation, networking and innovation were at the forefront of Hort Connections 2026 earlier this month at the Adelaide Convention Centre. The event brought together growers, supply chain partners, retailers, researchers and industry figures from across the global agriculture industry for a four-day programme of speaker sessions, trade shows, demonstrations and discussions. A panel dedicated to agricultural scholarships spoke about the importance of investing in capability and development, spotlighting programmes like the Agrifutures Horizon Scholarship, Australian Rural Leadership Programme and Nuffield Australia Scholarship. On the trade show floor, Daily Wine News had the chance to speak with primary suppliers of the winemaking and grapegrowing industry, including Visy, John Deere, Mottech Parkland, Yates Hort & Ag and Eurosolids.

The post Automation, networking and innovation spotlighted at Hort Connections 2026 appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
By Kyra Trussell

The topics of automation, networking and innovation were at the forefront of Hort Connections 2026 earlier this month at the Adelaide Convention Centre.

The event brought together growers, supply chain partners, retailers, researchers and industry figures from across the global agriculture industry for a four-day programme of speaker sessions, trade shows, demonstrations and discussions.

A panel dedicated to agricultural scholarships spoke on the importance of investing in capability and development, spotlighting programmes like the Agrifutures Horizon Scholarship, Australian Rural Leadership Programme and Nuffield Australia Scholarship.

James Haslett, an emerging Horizon Scholar, talked about how the possibilities for his future attracted him to the scholarship.

“A student wouldn’t really be able to have these opportunities and connect with industry without a little bit of additional help, so who knows – maybe five, 10, 15 years down the track after the conclusion of the programme – who knows what opportunities will present themselves? I find that really valuable,” Haslett said.

Julia Strang explained the Australian Rural Leadership Programme and how it can help leaders be better prepared to tackle industry challenges.

“It brings together about 30 leaders from different industries and communities, and it takes them through a series of intensive, real-world experiences, both across Australia and internationally.

“I think what makes it unique is its depth of experience. It’s not just about skills, it’s about developing self-awareness, building the ability to lead through complexity and creating a strong lifelong network of leaders shaping the future of regional Australia,” she said.

LTR: Courtney Lean and Julia Strang

Courtney Lean, one of the scholars undertaking the programme this year, said she is excited to start and encouraged others to “just apply”.

“I think I would really encourage anyone that’s looking at any of these programmes to challenge their own idea of what a leader is…you might not be in a typical leadership role or a management position or whatever that may look like to you in your field – but if you’re someone that’s excited, you’re engaged and passionate with what it is that you’re doing, you want to make change, you’re open-minded, and just willing to learn and grow, then please apply, because that’s the sort of person that they’re looking for. You’d get a lot out of it,” she said.

CEO of Nuffield Australia and 2015 scholar Andy Clarke spoke about how the programme allows scholars to find a problem they want to solve, then think of and present solutions.

“This is where a grower or a primary producer or somebody aligned to primary production has the ability to go and actually delve into something that’s really impactful for their sector and hopefully provide solutions coming back to bring forward as well,” he said.

On the trade show floor, Daily Wine News had the chance to speak with primary suppliers of the winemaking and grapegrowing industry.

Visy, who offers sustainable packaging solutions, was in attendance.

Sales manager Joshua Romanowicz said that the company is proud to support the industry.

“Visy is one of Australia’s largest privately owned family companies, and we pride ourselves on closing the loop for packaging in Australia. We believe materials are not recycled until they’ve been made into new products. We are investing to increase the recycled content in our glass, to get to an average of 70% recycled content,” he explained.

Romanowicz spoke of Visy’s latest development, its fibre-based insulation liner, Visycell, which is designed to replace EPS (polystyrene).

“Visycell is made from cardboard offcuts. We make an insulation pad, which lines the inside of a cardboard box and keeps the thermal properties there for e-commerce and long-haul deliveries. It’s an exciting innovation, and we continue to support the wine industry to be sustainable in the years to come.”

John Deere was also exhibiting at the event, showcasing its machinery and technological developments designed “to make operations more efficient”.

Erin Wagstaff, marketing manager at John Deere, spoke of the company’s cloud-based platform ‘Operations Centre’, which can store machine data, records and documentation for sprayers. The company also develops auto-steering systems alongside its well-known tractors.

“We love attending Hort Connections, I think it’s one of the best events on the horticultural calendar for the year. Talking to growers is one of the most important things for us. I think it’s really great for us to hear what’s on their mind, hear what’s going on in the industry, what are the trends that they’re getting on board with, so we can be aware and try to prepare for that.

“I think just the networking in general too, being able to keep up with those industry trends is really essential, and understanding what’s top of mind,” Wagstaff shared.

Andy Sawell from Mottech Parkland worked as an irrigation manager in a vineyard for eight years before starting his role with the irrigation systems provider.

LTR: Doron Halosh and Andy Sawell

“This role helped me understand what happens on the other side in the vineyard and really understand the needs, the trials and the issues that [vineyard irrigation] managers face…I understand how our products can really benefit the vineyards in that day-to-day process,” he revealed.

Mottech Parkland offers technology for automation of irrigation systems throughout the vineyard and wineries for wastewater distribution.

“The importance of automation going forward for wineries during this downturn is the labour saving, where we can save money for the business and reduce labour.

“Our technology allows vineyards to automate and also have feedback from sensors indicating that the block is actually on. It’s very useful during summer when you’re short-staffed, to know that your vineyard is being watered accordingly as you’ve programmed it to go.

“The new features of our Elite range offer a Wi-Fi network that enables Wi-Fi throughout the whole property, and the backup of that is in areas that have low phone signal, there is a safety option for your operators to use that Wi-Fi network to make calls and use the phone app to operate their irrigation,” Sawell detailed.

As for being at Hort Connections 2026, Sawell said the event was fantastic for showcasing the company’s new products to a whole range of audiences.

The downturn of the wine industry was a topic of conversation echoed by Christian Hubener, a French agronomist and crop consultant who manned the Eurosolids stand.

“In the past, wine was an easy thing to just produce and sell. Everybody was happy with it!”

Hubener spoke at length about the pressures climate change, declining consumption, changing consumer habits and the NoLo trend are placing on the industry.

“What we (Eurosolids) try to do is actually to help the grapegrowers to manage higher yield in order to have better balance in harvest and in the wine at the end of the season. The idea is really to introduce tools like fertiliser and biostimulants to achieve the goals that they might have with this new creation we’ve made for the wine industry,” he explained.

Chris Lloyd, McLaren Vale local and area sales manager for Yates Hort & Ag, expressed similar sentiments.

“I do feel for the industry, because as a whole, for many years now, the bulk side of the industry has added an excessive amount of pressure, and there’s multiple facets of the pressure, it’s not just one thing,” he shared.

Lloyd went on to explain the offerings from Yates Hort & Ag that can benefit grapegrowers, including its ‘Ecocarb PLUS’ – which can be used for powdery mildew suppression and as an eco-protector for botrytis.

He emphasised the importance of keeping plants healthy and using kelp to strengthen vines.

“The thinking should be if we keep kelp in the programme, we’re going to keep a healthy vine, we’re going to get healthy root systems, we’re going to be able to access more nutrition and water from a healthy root architecture.

“Healthy plants are less susceptible to insect and pest and disease attacks because of the strength of the cell walls, of the leaves – the most vulnerable part of the plant – and there’s the protection that the kelp brings to the vines that, not only do you get that increased efficacy of nutrition, but you can also get the benefits of reduction in use of pesticide and fungicide,” Lloyd emphasised.

Having attended Hort Connections at least three times prior to this year, he described the event as a key opportunity for the wholesale business to connect with its customer base of resellers at the event and catch up on any pending business.

“I always find that Hort Connections is the premiere event for the year because of the amount of people that it attracts, not only as exhibitors, but also as attendees. Being a local person, I get to see a lot of my mates in one place, so there’s always that good connection back to people that you may have not seen for a while – whether they be ex-employees or they’re just mates within the industry.

“We find it’s a great group of people and it’s a lot of camaraderie.”

title: “The results of a lifetime” – A ‘very special’ tasting event and the future of Gippsland wine
summary:
A wine tasting event set to uncork a collection of East Gippsland wines from the 1980s to the 2020 vintage will be held at the Nicholson River Winery’s barrel room this weekend. The event will explore the question, “Do East Gippsland wines age well?” across two sessions. Saturday will involve predominantly white and some red wines, while Sunday will focus exclusively on reds. The wines have mostly been made from grapes grown at Nicholson River, however, the occasional wine from other parts of Gippsland will also feature. Gippsland wine pioneer Ken Eckersley, who planted his first vines with his wife Juliet in 1978, described the event as an opportunity to experience “the results of a lifetime” of winemaking.

The post “The results of a lifetime” – A ‘very special’ tasting event and the future of Gippsland wine appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
The tastings, set to take place from 20-21 of June, will feature wines from as far back as the 80s. Image supplied

By Kyra Trussell

A wine tasting event set to uncork a collection of East Gippsland wines from the 1980s to the 2020 vintage will be held at the Nicholson River Winery’s barrel room this weekend.

The event will explore the question, “Do East Gippsland wines age well?” across two sessions. Saturday will involve predominantly white and some red wines, while Sunday will focus exclusively on reds.

The wines have mostly been made from grapes grown at Nicholson River, however, the occasional wine from other parts of Gippsland will also feature.

Gippsland wine pioneer Ken Eckersley, who planted his first vines with his wife Juliet in 1978, described the event as an opportunity to experience “the results of a lifetime” of winemaking.

“We’re getting too old now, and we’re looking to retire, so we’re looking back at over a lifetime of wine that we’ve made. I think we’ve improved our winemaking a lot over the years, I think it’s a very exciting area and we’ve been able to make reasonable alcohol, high flavour wines…it’s the results of a lifetime.

“These are wines I’ve kept in the cellar and I’m pulling them out over that weekend to a limited number of lucky people and we will taste them.

“This is sort of a history of Gippsland wines being tasted, it doesn’t get any better than that!” he exclaimed.

Eckersley will speak on each wine at the event, joking that “we’re spreading the gospel of wine – we’re missionaries here.”

The wine journey hasn’t been a walk in the park for the couple, with Eckersley expressing that the couple has had to “fight for the grapes”.

“We’ve had lots of pressures, sometimes drought, sometimes floods, plenty of birds –this is Australia, the home of birds – and so we’ve had to work hard. It hasn’t fallen into our lap; we don’t just sit under the vine and suck straws and watch the grapes grow,” he laughed.

As for what makes Gippsland wine so special, Eckersley highlighted its flavours.

“Gippsland is actually a big place, there are several different climate zones, and in the wine industry we’re fairly small, but I think what we have is really good flavours, distinctive flavours as our standout thing. That’s important, because I think a lot of Australian wines going down in flavour with the effect of climate change and so on.

“Here on the coast, where we’ve got a very mild climate, very long season, we get wonderful flavour development, we don’t have to leave them hanging on because they’re overripe.”

He added that people are willing to pay “very good money” for the grapes, as much as $8,000 a tonne.

When asked why the region is so underrated in his opinion, the winery owner said that the area “lacks confidence in itself, frankly”.

“It’s diverse, there’s timber, sheep, cattle, a little bit of horticulture, little bit of tourism, and it could do with a signature product, and wine has got that capacity, I think.

“I think the older areas, which were really chosen for Port and Sherry, are really suffering a lot from climate change. Here and in Tasmania, we’re in the same situation where the future is more likely to be here in these coastal places which are just starting to appear. People are just starting to notice that the future is more likely to lie with the newer areas than the old, tired areas.

“I think there’s a lot of potential here, for example, for more of the Italian varieties like Fiano and Vermentino and so on.”

He also pointed to the nature of the wine industry itself, emphasising its traditionalism that he believes tends to overshadow smaller regions.

“I think it’s a very conservative industry, too conservative, actually… I mean, it holds itself back, and I think there’s just been a failure to really notice or take up that what’s happening in the new areas in the last 30 to 40 years. It’s a highly competitive industry with the big boys and the big areas, traditional ones.

“I think South Australia is pretty insulated from what’s happening in wine and the rest of Australia, frankly. Everyone in Adelaide thinks they’re the centre of the wine universe, and that’s really delusional.

“There is a big wine world!” he concluded.

Tickets and more information on the tasting event are available via the East Gippsland Winter Festival website.

title: Château Tanunda secures two Top 10 spots in global Shiraz ranking
summary:
Barossa’s Château Tanunda has had two of its flagship wines ranked among the Top 10 Best Shiraz in the World at the Syrah du Monde 2026 competition in France. Grand Barossa Shiraz 2024 and 100 Year Old Vines Shiraz 2022 made the ranking, beating out international entries from 20 wine-producing nations and earning two Gold medals. “Château Tanunda's success not only reflects the exceptional quality of its wines but also reinforces the Barossa's reputation as one of the world's leading regions for Shiraz,” said managing director Michelle Geber.

The post Château Tanunda secures two Top 10 spots in global Shiraz ranking appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
Château Tanunda has received global recognition via the ranking. Image supplied

Barossa’s Château Tanunda has had two of its flagship wines ranked among the Top 10 Best Shiraz in the World at the Syrah du Monde 2026 competition in France.

Grand Barossa Shiraz 2024 and 100 Year Old Vines Shiraz 2022 made the ranking, beating out international entries from 20 wine-producing nations and earning two Gold medals.

“Château Tanunda’s success not only reflects the exceptional quality of its wines but also reinforces the Barossa’s reputation as one of the world’s leading regions for Shiraz,” said managing director Michelle Geber.

“The Grand Barossa range is one of our most internationally recognised collections, so receiving recognition at a global competition such as Syrah du Monde is incredibly rewarding. Achievements like this continue to build Australia’s reputation as a producer of exceptional Shiraz.”

The winery earlier this year released the Grand Barossa Shiraz 2024 in a new 375ml format in a bid to “suit a wider range of consumers and occasions”.

Château Tanunda said that its 100 Year Old Vines Shiraz 2022 symbolises its “commitment to preserving and championing Barossa’s unique old-vine heritage”. The winery also added that, as a “proud member” of the Francs de Pied association, it supports the recognition and protection of rare own-rooted vineyards around the world.

The Château Tanunda 100 Year Old Vines Shiraz 2022 Retails for RRP $180, while the Grand Barossa Shiraz 2024 retails for RRP $25. The wines are available for purchase online via the winery’s website or in-person nationally at fine wine retailers.

title: Misha’s Vineyard Wines announces sale of Bendigo Vineyard, assures “business as usual”
summary:
Misha's Vineyard Wines has announced the sale of its 57-hectare Bendigo vineyard property. The winery said this decision marks “an important new phase” for the Central Otago wine producer “while ensuring continuity of wine production through a multi-year grape supply agreement”. Under the terms of the agreement, Misha's Vineyard Wines will continue to receive grapes from the Bendigo vineyard, which has been at the heart of the company’s wines since its inception, under a multi-year supply arrangement. According to Misha’s Vineyard Wines, the transaction will enable the company to focus on crafting and marketing its premium Central Otago wines which are reportedly enjoyed in 16 markets around the world. "This is a positive and strategic step for our business," said Andy Wilkinson, co-founder and director of Misha's Vineyard Wines.

The post Misha’s Vineyard Wines announces sale of Bendigo Vineyard, assures “business as usual” appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
The Bendigo vineyard has been sold. Image courtesy Misha’s Vineyard Wines via Facebook

Misha’s Vineyard Wines has announced the sale of its 57-hectare Bendigo vineyard property.

The winery said this decision marks “an important new phase” for the Central Otago wine producer “while ensuring continuity of wine production through a multi-year grape supply agreement”.

Under the terms of the agreement, Misha’s Vineyard Wines will continue to receive grapes from the Bendigo vineyard, which has been at the heart of the company’s wines since its inception, under a multi-year supply arrangement.

According to Misha’s Vineyard Wines, the transaction will enable the company to focus on crafting and marketing its premium Central Otago wines which are reportedly enjoyed in 16 markets around the world.

“This is a positive and strategic step for our business,” said Andy Wilkinson, co-founder and director of Misha’s Vineyard Wines.

“Our focus has always been on producing exceptional wines and building our brand in markets around the world. This transaction allows us to continue doing exactly that, while working alongside experienced wine industry owners who share our commitment to quality and the long-term future of the vineyard.”

Wilkinson believes the sale reflects confidence in the future of New Zealand’s premium wine sector.

“We are delighted with the outcome and have every confidence in the new owners as custodians of the vineyard.

“Their experience in producing premium New Zealand wines and commitment to quality gives us confidence that the vineyard will continue to thrive for many years to come.”

The winery said that for customers and trade partners, it’s business as usual. The company affirmed that it will continue to operate independently, producing and marketing its wines under the Misha’s Vineyard brand as well as “maintaining its relationships with customers and distribution partners around the world”.

“The vineyard has always been an important part of our story, and that story continues.

“As we celebrate our 20th vintage, our customers can look forward to the same wines, the same commitment to quality, and the same passion that has defined Misha’s Vineyard Wines from the beginning,” Wilkinson reassured.

title: Central Otago’s organics shift celebrated at national awards
summary:
Central Otago’s shift towards organic wine has been recognised at the recent Aotearoa New Zealand Organic Wine Awards, with several of the region’s wineries receiving accolades. Of the 181 medals awarded, 73 went to 11 Central Otago wineries, making for 40% of the national total. Gibbston Valley was a strong performer at the awards, securing six gold medals and the highest total medal count. Gibbston Valley chief winemaker Christopher Keys said the results reflect both vineyard excellence and the strong trajectory of the wider industry. “It is a joy to see the consistent excellence New Zealand’s organic vineyards have reached. “This is recognition not just for Gibbston Valley, but for New Zealand organic wine as a whole. It’s one part of a bigger picture, but it is an important part,” Keys said.

The post Central Otago’s organics shift celebrated at national awards appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
Christopher Keys, chief winemaker at Gibbston Valley. Images supplied

Central Otago’s shift towards organic wine has been recognised at the recent Aotearoa New Zealand Organic Wine Awards, with several of the region’s wineries receiving accolades.

Of the 181 medals awarded, 73 went to 11 Central Otago wineries, making for 40% of the national total.

Gibbston Valley was a strong performer at the awards, securing six gold medals and the highest total medal count.

Gibbston Valley chief winemaker Christopher Keys said the results reflect both vineyard excellence and the strong trajectory of the wider industry.

“It is a joy to see the consistent excellence New Zealand’s organic vineyards have reached.

“This is recognition not just for Gibbston Valley, but for New Zealand organic wine as a whole. It’s one part of a bigger picture, but it is an important part,” Keys said.

Central Otago Winegrowers Association general manager Carolyn Murray welcomed the success of Central Otago producers at the awards.

“With 30% of our vineyard area now under organic management, Central Otago has become New Zealand’s leading organic wine region.

“This achievement reflects the commitment of growers who believe that great wine begins in the vineyard – with healthy soils, balanced ecosystems, and a deep respect for the land,” she said.

According to the 2025 New Zealand Winegrowers Sustainability Report, 16 per cent of wineries now hold organic certification, up from 10 per cent in 2024.

Keys said this growth is “translating into quality and character in the glass”.

“Being organic brings you closer to the place, revealing the real identity of the wine. When you open a bottle, it has the ability to transport you back to this special part of the world.”

Now in its 13th year, the Aotearoa New Zealand Organic Wine Awards celebrate the country’s best certified organic wines. To be eligible, entrants must use certified organic grapes and are assessed through a blind tasting process by “an independent, consumer-based judging panel”.

Gibbston Valley’s top honours were led by its single-vineyard Pinot Noir’s, which featured strongly in the gold medal tally, alongside the 2025 rosé and 2024 Chardonnay.

Other major award winners included Greystone as ‘Riedel Vineyard of the Year’, Mount Edward Muirkirk pinot noir 2024 as ‘Riedel Wine of the Show’ and Quartz Reef Wines as ‘Sustainable Vineyard of the Year’.

Gibbston Valley executive managing director Cristina Griffith said the results underscore the winery’s long-term investment in organic practices.

“These awards highlight not only how outstanding the wines are, but also the commitment behind converting our vineyards without losing an ounce of quality.”

content:
LTR: Alex Coombs from Craggy Range and Heath Miller from Pask. Images supplied

Heath Miller has been announced as the 2026 Osmond Nurseries Young Viticulturist of the Year for Hawke’s Bay, whilst Alex Coombs took out the title in North Island.

It is the third time Miller has entered the competition, a feat that New Zealand Winegrowers (NZW) said highlights his determination.

Nicky Grandorge, leadership & communities manager at NZW, said that the organisation loves seeing contestants come back year after year.

“It highlights the passion they have to progress themselves and the importance of the competition,” Grandorge said.

The organisation also extended congratulations to runner ups Thomas Mackenzie from Indevin and Thalia Osborne from Ash Ridge Wines. Mackenzie was the first person to register for the 2026 competitions across the entire country back in January, whilst Osborne represented Hawke’s Bay in last year’s Young Winemaker national final.

Contestants Harsh Bhagran, Ross Graham and Mikaela Salmon were also congratulated for “tackling the challenges with gusto”.

It was also North Island winner Alex Coombs’ third year competing in the competition.

Coombs’ participation followed a year overseas, which reportedly equipped him with more viticultural experience and a “strong focus and determination to take out the title”.

NZW congratulated second-place winner Gagandeep Singh as well as the other two contestants Phattaradanai Wannaphirun (Neil) and Santiago Mendez Gasca for their efforts.

“All four contestants shone in various sections of the competition highlighting their personal strengths,” New Zealand Winegrowers said.

Coombs won both the head-to-head races, the Ecotrellis trellising section, the BioStart Hortisports and the Syngenta Biologicals Nutrition section.

From this regional competition, Miller and Coombs both won a Fruitfed Supplies Field Trip plus $1,000 each and will both compete for “even greater prizes” at the National Final.
